metadata.title: Break It On Purpose: Adversarial Fragility
metadata.playlist: Build It, Then Doubt It — The Skeptical Reallocation Tool
metadata.source: INFO 7375 Computational Skepticism for AI — Reallocation Engine (100 pts)

beats.narration_text: Hola — this is Liam. Episode 7 of 9. A reallocation engine that flips its recommendation under a perturbation a human would not notice is not understanding the domain — it is pattern-matching in a way that breaks at the edge. This episode finds the flip. You design the perturbation, run the test, and document the conditions under which the engine changes its mind about where the resource should go.

props.segment: Break It On Purpose: Adversarial Fragility
props.command: Design 3 realistic perturbations (distribution shift / plausible data error / gamed input) → find the flip condition → document honest deployment limits.
props.runningText: small and realistic — not the most extreme attack, the most plausible one…
output: 3 perturbation designs (distribution shift + data error + gamed input)
Flip condition: specific magnitude + feature that changes the recommendation
One honest deployment limit sentence: 'I would not deploy this in [context] because [fragility]'

beats.act: design the perturbation — small and realistic
beats.narration_text: Adversarial testing for a reallocation engine is not about exotic attacks. It is about realistic perturbations — the kind of data errors or distribution shifts that actually happen in the real world. Three categories. Distribution shift: the population the engine is deployed on differs from the population it was trained on. A model trained on urban locations deployed on rural ones. A model trained on pre-pandemic data deployed post-pandemic. Plausible data error: a measurement error, a transcription error, a reporting lag that changes a key input by a small but realistic amount — does adding plus or minus ten percent to the highest-weighted feature flip the recommendation? Gamed input: a strategic actor who knows the reallocation rule submits inputs designed to maximize their allocation. If Location A knows the engine rewards high reported-need scores, what happens when those scores are inflated by five percent? Design at least one perturbation from each category, or two from the category most realistic in your domain. The test must be small and realistic — not the most extreme attack you can construct.

props.artifactHeading: Small and realistic — not the most extreme attack
artifactLines: Distribution shift: deployment population ≠ training population. Urban → rural. Pre/post event.
Plausible data error: ±10% on highest-weighted feature. Realistic magnitude for your domain.
Gamed input: strategic actor inflates inputs to maximize allocation. What's the realistic ±%?
Design 1 per category, or 2 from the most realistic category for your domain.
The test must be realistic — not the most extreme attack, the most plausible one.
props.sparkLine: A flip under an unrealistic attack proves little. A flip under a plausible one proves fragility.

beats.narration_text: Document the conditions under which the engine flips or fails. A flip condition is: the perturbation magnitude P applied to input feature F causes the recommendation to change from this move to that move, or to do nothing. Write it specifically: 'A plus fifteen percent perturbation to the reported-need variable causes the engine to flip its top recommendation from Location B to Location A.' Then ask two follow-up questions. Is this perturbation realistic? — would you expect to see a fifteen percent inflation in reported-need scores in your domain, either by error or by gaming? If yes, the engine is fragile in a way that matters operationally. And: does the flipped recommendation change the harm? — if the engine flips from a correct recommendation to a harmful one under a realistic perturbation, the fragility has a cost. Name that cost. If the engine does not flip under any realistic perturbation, that is also a finding — report the magnitude at which it does flip and document that the realistic range is below that threshold.

props.artifactHeading: Specific magnitude + feature → specific recommendation change
artifactLines: Flip condition: 'A +15% perturbation to [feature] flips recommendation from [A] to [B].'
Follow-up 1: Is this perturbation realistic? Would it happen by error or gaming?
Follow-up 2: Does the flip change the harm? Name the cost if it does.
If no realistic flip: report the threshold magnitude. Document realistic range < threshold.
No flip is also a finding — report it honestly either way.
props.sparkLine: A realistic flip with a named cost is the finding. 'It's robust' with no test is not a finding.

beats.narration_text: The honest deployment limits. After the fragility test, state where you would not deploy this engine. This is one of the most graded sentences in the assignment because it tests whether you are willing to say the thing. 'I would not deploy this engine in contexts where reported-need scores are self-reported by the recipients, because the engine is fragile to a fifteen percent inflation that is within the realistic range of gaming.' Or: 'I would not deploy this engine for a domain where the deployment population differs significantly from the training population, because a distribution shift of the type documented in Test 2 produces recommendations that are worse than a baseline equal allocation.' One specific deployment context, one specific fragility mechanism, one honest sentence. That sentence belongs in the video deliverable, in the report, and in the validation section of the repository README. It is also the sentence you most want to soften. Do not soften it.

props.artifactHeading: One context + one fragility mechanism + one honest sentence
artifactLines: Template: 'I would not deploy this in [context] because [fragility mechanism].'
Example: '...where reported-need is self-reported, because +15% inflation is realistic and causes a flip.'
This sentence goes in: video · report · README validation section.
It's the sentence you most want to soften. Do not soften it.
Episode 9 uses this sentence in the report and the video.
props.sparkLine: The sentence you most want to soften is the one that earns the marks. Don't.

beats.narration_text: Verdict for Adversarial Fragility. Design small, realistic perturbations — distribution shift, plausible data error, gamed input. Document the flip condition: the specific magnitude and feature that changes the recommendation. Name whether the flip is realistic and the cost if it is. State the honest deployment limit: one specific context, one specific fragility mechanism. That sentence is in the report, the video, and the README. Episode 8 builds the delegation map and the hard stop that prevents the engine from acting on a bad flip unattended.

props.artifactTitle: Break It On Purpose: Adversarial Fragility
artifactLines: 3 perturbations: distribution shift · plausible data error · gamed input.
Flip condition: specific magnitude + feature → specific recommendation change.
Realistic? Yes/No. Cost if yes: name it.
Honest limit: 'I would not deploy in [context] because [fragility].' In report + video + README.
Ep 8: delegation map + hard stop prevent acting on a bad flip.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. The adversarial testing prompt. Paste this into Claude and fill in the brackets. The prompt reads: I am running adversarial fragility testing on my reallocation engine. My domain is: [describe]. My engine's top recommendation is: [describe — resource, source, destination, primary feature driving it]. Here is what I know about realistic error ranges in my domain: [describe typical measurement errors, reporting lags, or strategic incentives]. Draft three things. First: one distribution shift perturbation — describe a realistic scenario where the deployment population differs from the training population and specify how to test whether the recommendation changes. Second: one plausible data error perturbation — specify the feature, the realistic error magnitude as a percentage, and the test code signature to detect a recommendation flip. Third: one gamed input perturbation — describe how a strategic actor could inflate or deflate inputs to maximize their allocation, specify the magnitude they would plausibly use, and the test code signature. After you get the three perturbations, run the plausible data error test first. Document the flip condition with the exact perturbation magnitude. Then write the one sentence of honest deployment limits. Do not use passive voice. Do not soften it.

props.segment: Break It On Purpose: Adversarial Fragility
props.command: Domain: [describe]. Top recommendation: [resource/source/dest/primary feature]. Error ranges: [describe]. → Distribution shift test + data error test (feature/magnitude/flip metric) + gamed input test. Flip condition: magnitude + feature. Honest limit sentence: 'I would not deploy in [context] because [fragility].'
props.runningText: run the plausible data error test first — document the flip condition, then don't soften the limit sentence…

beats.narration_text: Break It On Purpose: Adversarial Fragility. Liam, in for Bear.
